content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED
by: ISAIS(c)(15)

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

May 10, 1982

Donna Dascenzo
Senate Rules Committee
State Capitol, 4th Floor
Building West
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Advice Letter
No. A-82-082

Dear Ms. Dascenzo:

Jeanne Pritchard has asked me to respond to your telephone
inquiry on behalf of candidates who, in their campaign literature,
urge voters to vote for or against particular ballot measures.
You have asked whether such candidates are required to report
any part of the payment for their campaign literature as an
independent or in-kind expenditure for or against the ballot
measures.

Ordinarily, so long as a candidate is acting independently
of any ballot measure committee, expenditures in connection
with campaign literature which advocates a particular position
on a ballot measure are not in-kind contributions or independent
expenditures for or against the ballot measure. It is an
integral part of a candidate's campaign to take a position,
or to urge others to take a position, on significant public
issues, including ballot measures.

There may, however, be some circumstances in which a
candidate's campaign literature advocating a position on a
ballot measure would constitute an independent expenditure
or an in-kind contribution related to the measure. For
example, if the campaign literature were prepared at
at the behest of, in coordination with, or under the direction
and control of a ballot measure committee, there would probably
be reportable in-kind contributions to the ballot measure
committee. In addition, costs incurred in connection with
campaign literature expressly advocating support or defeat
of a ballot measure, which was developed, produced and distributed
totally independently of a ballot measure committee, might,
in unusual circumstances, constitute an independent expenditure
on behalf of the ballot measure. This could occur if the

Donna Dascenzo
May 10, 1982
Page 2


issues or constituencies involved in the ballot measure are
distinctly different from those involved in the candidate's
election campaign.

     I hope this answers your questions.  If you wish to
discuss this further, or want more specific advice on specific
literature, please feel free to call me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:plh

State of California

Memorandum

To : Barbara                                                    Date : 4/22/82


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Jeanne

Subject:


         Question from Donna Dicenzo, Senate Rules Committee:
         (phone: 5-0924)


              If a candidate is sending out a piece of campaign
         literature for his campaign, and asks the readers to
         vote a particular way on a ballot measure, or a number
         of ballot measures, and he does this independent of any
         control or direction by any committee, has the candidate
         made an independent expenditure?

              This is a "general" question she has gotten from
         a number of people.


                                        [illegible]

sections.question:
from Donna Dicenzo, Senate Rules Committee: 
(phone: 5-0924) 
If a candidate is sending out a piece of campaign 
literature for his campaign, and asks the readers to 
vote a particular way on a ballot measure, or a number 
of ballot measures, and he does this indeDendent of any 
control or direction by any committee, has the candidate 
made an independent expenditure? 
This is a "general" question she has gotten from 
a number of people.
sections.conclusion: Ordinarily, so long as a candidate is acting independently of any ballot measure committee, expenditures in connection with campaign literature which advocates a particular position on a ballot measure are not in-kind contributions or independent expenditures for or against the ballot measure. It is an integral part of a candidate's campaign to take a position, or to urge others to take a position, on significant public issues, including ballot measures. However, if the campaign literature were prepared or distributed at the behest of, in coordination with, or under the direction and control of a ballot measure committee, there would probably be reportable in-kind contributions to the ballot measure committee. In addition, costs incurred in connection with campaign literature expressly advocating support or defeat of a ballot measure, which was developed, produced and distributed totally independently of a ballot measure committee, might, in unusual circumstances, constitute an independent expenditure on behalf of the ballot measure if the issues or constituencies involved in the ballot measure are distinctly different from those involved in the candidate's election campaign.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether candidates who advocate positions on ballot measures in their campaign literature must report such expenditures as independent or in-kind expenditures related to those ballot measures.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Candidate campaign literature advocating positions on ballot measures generally need not be reported as independent or in-kind expenditures, as it is integral to a candidate's campaign. However, reportable expenditures may arise if the literature is coordinated with a ballot measure committee or if the ballot measure issues are distinctly separate from the candidate's election campaign.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the formal advice letter from Barbara A. Mailman (Chief, Legal Division) dated May 10, 1982, and the internal memorandum from Jeanne to Barbara dated 4/22/82 that prompted the response. OCR quality is good. Letter number A-82-082 confirmed in document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: from Donna Dicenzo, Senate Rules Committee: 
(phone: 5-0924) 
If a candidate is sending out a piece of campaign 
literature for his campaign, and asks the readers to 
vote a particular way on a ballot measure, or a number 
of ballot measures, and he does this indeDendent of any 
control or direction by any committee, has the candidate 
made an independent expenditure? 
This is a "general" question she has gotten from 
a number of people.

CONCLUSION: Ordinarily, so long as a candidate is acting independently of any ballot measure committee, expenditures in connection with campaign literature which advocates a particular position on a ballot measure are not in-kind contributions or independent expenditures for or against the ballot measure. It is an integral part of a candidate's campaign to take a position, or to urge others to take a position, on significant public issues, including ballot measures. However, if the campaign literature were prepared or distributed at the behest of, in coordination with, or under the direction and control of a ballot measure committee, there would probably be reportable in-kind contributions to the ballot measure committee. In addition, costs incurred in connection with campaign literature expressly advocating support or defeat of a ballot measure, which was developed, produced and distributed totally independently of a ballot measure committee, might, in unusual circumstances, constitute an independent expenditure on behalf of the ballot measure if the issues or constituencies involved in the ballot measure are distinctly different from those involved in the candidate's election campaign.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether candidates must report campaign literature advocating positions on ballot measures as independent or in-kind expenditures related to those measures. The response clarifies that such reporting is generally not required when the candidate acts independently, but exceptions exist for coordinated efforts with ballot measure committees or distinctly separate ballot issues.
